sketchBlocks.title: Understanding Boolean Operators
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Boolean operators allow us to combine multiple conditions in programming. The three main operators are **AND**, **OR**, and **NOT**. 

**AND** returns True only if both conditions are True. 
**OR** returns True if at least one condition is True. 
**NOT** inverts the truth value of a condition. 

**Example:** 
```python
age = 20
if age >= 18 and age < 65:
    print('Eligible for work')
``` 
In this example, the message will print only if both conditions are met. 

**Your turn:** Try changing the age value and see how it affects the output.

sketchBlocks.title: Using Boolean Logic in Conditions
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When writing conditions, you can combine them using boolean logic. For instance, you can check if a number is both greater than 10 and less than 20 using the **AND** operator. 

**Example:** 
```python
number = 15
if number > 10 and number < 20:
    print('Number is between 10 and 20')
``` 
This will print the message if the number is indeed between 10 and 20. 

**Try it yourself:** Modify the number and see what happens!

quizDraft.title: Understanding AND Operator
quizDraft.prompt:
What will be the output of the following code?
```python
x = 5
if x > 3 and x < 10:
    print('In range')
else:
    print('Out of range')
```
quizDraft.hint: Consider both conditions in the if statement.

help.concept: The AND operator requires both conditions to be true.
help.hint_1: Check if x is greater than 3.
help.hint_2: Now check if x is less than 10.

quizDraft.title: Understanding OR Operator
quizDraft.prompt:
What will be the output of the following code?
```python
x = 5
if x < 3 or x > 10:
    print('Out of range')
else:
    print('In range')
```
quizDraft.hint: Think about what OR requires to return true.

help.concept: The OR operator returns true if at least one condition is true.
